narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 78.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. As a male student from a white background planning to attend full-time with a middle-family income, your profile includes a Year 1 school decile ranking of 2, a Year 3 decile of 2, an LSAT score of 36, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.80. These details were compared to typical students, where average Year 1 deciles are around 5.7, Year 3 around 5.5, LSAT scores about 36.7, and GPAs near 3.21—meaning your academic rankings and scores fall notably below the norm that often predicts bar passage. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile ranking, exerting nearly equal influence in the same unfavorable direction. Your LSAT score came next in importance, followed by your family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status—all contributing to the failure prediction, though with gradually less impact down the list. While your gender, race, full-time plans, and income align closely with the majority of students who pass, they still nudged the outcome slightly negative in this case. In contrast, stronger students typically have higher deciles and GPAs that pull predictions toward success. To improve your future chances, focus on boosting your academic performance, such as aiming for higher decile rankings through better grades in any further studies. Retaking the LSAT to exceed the average could also help shift the balance. Building a stronger undergraduate record, if possible through additional coursework, might address that key shortfall too. With targeted effort on these changeable areas, you could present a more favorable profile next time and increase your odds of bar success. Keep pushing forward—you have the potential to turn this around.
